coredata.dc:title: Biomass valorization of liquid whey into carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal process for food pathogenic bactericidal activity and photocatalytic degradation of brilliant red dye
coredata.dc:creator: Thakur, Sweezee
Bains, Aarti
Kumar, Anil
Goksen, Gulden
Yaqoob, Mudasir
Parvez, Mohammad Khalid
Al-Dosari, Mohammed S.
Chawla, Prince
coredata.dc:description: Whey is the primary waste material of the dairy industries; therefore, the present study focuses on the valorization of milk processing industry-originated liquid whey into valuable products, specifically liquid whey carbon quantum dots (LW-CQDs) for pathogenic bactericidal and photocatalytic dye reduction efficacy. Facile synthesis of fluorescent LW-CQDs was carried out by employing a green hydrothermal approach at 200 °C for 12 h. Several analytical techniques were used to confirm the thermally stable spherical particles, measuring 9.04 ± 0.76 nm size in diameter, primarily composed of carbon and oxygen LW-CQDs. The presence of diverse functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and methyl groups) of LW-CQDs contributed to overall optical properties, which manifested a blue emission peak at 418 nm with 240 nm excitation wavelength in fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–Visible spectra featured two peaks (π → π ∗ transition of C = C bonds and n → π ∗ transition of C = O bonds) at around 249 and 293 nm, respectively. Moreover, the synthesized LW-CQDs exhibited a significantly higher zone of inhibition (25.98 ± 0.17 mm) and significantly lower minimum inhibitory concentration (4.47 ± 0.01 μl/ml) against the Staphylococcus aureus. In addition, LW-CQDs revealed a higher killing rate of reaction for S. aureus as compared to K. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa, and S. typhi and remarkably degraded 92.95 % of brilliant red dye under visible light (2000 lux). Hence, these facile LW-CQDs hold potential for applications in the effectiveness of antimicrobial and photocatalytic dye reduction activity, which show valuable contributions to both waste valorization and sustainable material development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye reduction
Photoluminescence
Antimicrobial
Spectroscopy
Sensors
Circular economy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from liquid whey
Particle size and zeta potential of LW-CQDs
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of LW-CQDs
Time kill kinetic study of LW-CQDs
Particle size, PDI (poly dispersity index), and zeta potential
Fluorescence spectroscopy and stability of LW-CQDs
Time kill kinetic study of LW-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was funded through the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R379), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.Authors are thankful to the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R379), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for supporting this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of LW-CQDs (Liquid whey-derived Carbon quantum dots). (A) UV-visible spectrum; (B) average particle size; (C) zeta potential; (D) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy; (E) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy; (F) energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy mapping; (G) differential scanning calorimetry; (H) fluorescence emission spectra and (I) Fluorescence stability. Data are presented as means ± SD (n = 3).

Antimicrobial efficacy of LW-CQDs against K. pneumoniae, S. typhi, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus: (A) Zone of inhibition, and (B) MIC and MBC. The results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation of >3 independent replicates. The mean value with different lowercase (a-d) alphabets within the column represents significantly different values from each other which was analyzed based on the ANOVA (analysis of variance) and post hoc test.

Photocatalytic Brilliant red dye reduction by liquid whey-derived carbon quantum dots (LW-CQDs) after 30 min: (A) UV-visible spectra of dye under visible light, (B) % Degradation of Brilliant red dye. Data are presented as means ± SD (n = 3).
Time kill kinetic to study the effect of LW-CQDs against pathogenic bacteria.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a-bMeans within the row with different lowercase superscripts are significantly different (p < 0.05) from each other.
A−DMeans within the column with different uppercase superscripts are significantly different (p < 0.05) from each other.
1The data are presented as mean values with standard deviations (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Biomass valorization of liquid whey into carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal process for food pathogenic bactericidal activity and photocatalytic degradation of brilliant red dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2457, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacognosy, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2457, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Whey is the primary waste material of the dairy industries; therefore, the present study focuses on the valorization of milk processing industry-originated liquid whey into valuable products, specifically liquid whey carbon quantum dots (LW-CQDs) for pathogenic bactericidal and photocatalytic dye reduction efficacy. Facile synthesis of fluorescent LW-CQDs was carried out by employing a green hydrothermal approach at 200 °C for 12 h. Several analytical techniques were used to confirm the thermally stable spherical particles, measuring 9.04 ± 0.76 nm size in diameter, primarily composed of carbon and oxygen LW-CQDs. The presence of diverse functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and methyl groups) of LW-CQDs contributed to overall optical properties, which manifested a blue emission peak at 418 nm with 240 nm excitation wavelength in fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–Visible spectra featured two peaks (π → π ∗ transition of C = C bonds and n → π ∗ transition of C = O bonds) at around 249 and 293 nm, respectively. Moreover, the synthesized LW-CQDs exhibited a significantly higher zone of inhibition (25.98 ± 0.17 mm) and significantly lower minimum inhibitory concentration (4.47 ± 0.01 μl/ml) against the Staphylococcus aureus. In addition, LW-CQDs revealed a higher killing rate of reaction for S. aureus as compared to K. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa, and S. typhi and remarkably degraded 92.95 % of brilliant red dye under visible light (2000 lux). Hence, these facile LW-CQDs hold potential for applications in the effectiveness of antimicrobial and photocatalytic dye reduction activity, which show valuable contributions to both waste valorization and sustainable material development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The effective utilization of food waste presents a significant opportunity for the development of sustainable practices and the promotion of a circular economy (Lagioia et al., 2024). Food waste is rich in diverse biomolecules, including vitamins, polyphenols, polysaccharides, proteins, and natural colorants, lipids, and oils (Panja et al., 2023). These biomolecules contain various functional groups such as hydroxyl, thiol, amino, and carboxyl groups, which contribute to their versatile properties and industrial applications (Wang et al., 2024). The circular economy concept, emphasizes the sustainable use of resources and utilizes food waste as a raw material (Subbarao et al., 2023). Nowadays researchers focus on chemical or physical modifications of these biomaterials and this leads to significant changes in the morphological and chemical transformation to make them suitable for reuse in a wide range of applications, including pollutant sensing and adsorption (Kour et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023). The significant improvement of these biomaterials helps in the facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with several advantageous properties including their small nanoscale size, chemical stability, cost effectiveness, excellent biocompatibility, aqueous dispersibility, and high photoluminescence properties (Palacio-Vergara et al., 2023; Pourmadadi et al., 2023).

Furthermore, the unique physiochemical properties of CQDs, such as easy synthesis, thermal stability, and high specific surface area, have gained significant attention in various fields including target drug delivery, sensing and biosensing, photocatalytic degradation, and energy storage (Reza et al., 2023). CQDs with a preferred size of 10 nm, stand out as zero-dimensional nanomaterials with exceptional characteristics and the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs using natural materials has gained popularity due to its environmentally friendly nature, often referred to as green chemistry (Giordano et al., 2023; Quan et al., 2023). While organic molecules are commonly used for producing high-quality CQDs with desirable quantum yields, the toxicity associated with aromatic hydrocarbon precursors has limited their widespread application (Rawat et al., 2023). To overcome this challenge, there has been a shift towards utilizing natural food waste as a potential source for synthesizing CQDs (John et al., 2023). Various food waste materials, including vegetables, nuts, fruits, meat byproducts, milk, and other waste products, are being explored for their renewable, cost-effective, non-toxic, and biocompatible properties (L. Wang et al., 2023). This approach not only contributes to sustainable practices but also addresses concerns related to the environmental impact and health risks associated with certain synthetic precursors. CQDs have been successfully synthesized using various sources related to dairy, such as milk, milk proteins, expired milk, and yogurt. Notably, the utilization of dairy industrial waste for the production of specific bio-nanomaterials has gained significance, offering potential advantages for both industrial applications and consumer use (Gao et al., 2023). Whey, a common yellowish/greenish by-product generated during cheese/paneer production is produced globally at an annual scale exceeding 160 million tons. Although, it is often considered waste in the majority of the food industry (Murata et al). Its composition comprises water, proteins, lactic acid, lactose, fat, and minerals (Brenda et al., 2023). In the context of green synthesis of CQDs, extensive surface doping is often unnecessary because the precursors already encompass a wide array of compounds, such as proteins, sugars, underutilized resources, lipids, and other vitamins (Chowmasundaram et al., 2023). This approach not only contributes to reducing environmental pollution but also enhances the value of CQDs. To the best of our knowledge, limited research has explored the utilization of liquid whey for CQD synthesis. Liquid whey has gained interest due to its abundance, cost-effectiveness, and the potential environmental benefits associated with repurposing waste materials (Poonia & Pandey, 2023).

In recent times, infectious diseases caused by various microorganisms, including viruses, fungi, parasites, and other pathogens, have significantly impacted public health globally, and continue to be a leading global cause of mortality (Murali et al., 2023). Moreover, recent studies have illustrated that CQDs exhibit effectiveness in targeting a wide range of microorganisms, including both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Luo et al., 2023). Water contamination and environmental pollution have become critical global issues, primarily driven by the release of untreated or inadequately treated colored wastewater into water bodies (Sewwandi et al., 2023). Dyes and organic pollutants can exert detrimental effects on human health, aquatic ecosystems, and the environment. Therefore, the treatment of colored contaminated effluents is critical to ensure the provision of clean and safe water (Hassan et al., 2023). The presence of carbonyl groups on the surface of CQDs is essential for enhancing their photocatalytic activity. These carbonyl groups play a crucial role in facilitating the separation and transfer of electrons and holes, which are fundamental processes in photocatalysis. By promoting these processes, CQDs can effectively degrade and remove dyes and organic pollutants from water (Gul et al., 2023).

In the previous studies, researchers used food waste precursors and hydrothermal approach for synthesis of CQDs with limited information on various important analytical techniques including dynamic light scattering, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, and differential scanning calorimetry to characterize the CQDs. Also, limited information is available on killing kinetics and photocatalytic dye degradation efficacy under visible light, therefore, this study primarily emphasizes the thermal stability, evaluation of killing kinetics and dye degradation efficacy of LW-CQDs. In addition, the key objectives include the feasibility of utilizing liquid whey, for the green synthesis of CQDs via a hydrothermal process, emphasizing waste valorization and sustainability. As well, as characterization of the CQDs to determine their structural, optical, and chemical properties and to evaluate the bactericidal efficacy against foodborne pathogens, aiming to enhance food safety. Furthermore, to assess the photocatalytic performance of CQDs in degrading synthetic brilliant red dye, in the context of environmental remediation efforts. Overall, the hydrothermal valorization of liquid whey into CQDs represents a significant advancement in green chemistry, offering multiple utilizations in addressing foodborne pathogens and environmental pollution through photocatalytic dye degradation.

Raw material i.e. Liquid whey was collected from the Verka Milk Plant, Jalandhar, (31°21′56″N 75°33′32″ E), Punjab, India. Fresh whey was filtered through two folded muslin cloths packed in air-tight glass containers and stored at refrigerated temperature (4–7 °C) till further use. The study used reagents of analytical grade without any additional purification steps. For the experimental procedures, ethanol of 99.5 % purity and sodium hydroxide of 99 % purity were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. The syringe filter (0.22 μm) of PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene) material, a syringe of polyvinyl chloride material, and a dialysis membrane (3 KDa) were purchased from Cole-Parmer, Dispo-Van®, and Merck Millipore, Mumbai, India, respectively. Brilliant red dye (99 % purity) from Hi-Media Pvt. Ltd. was obtained from Mumbai, India. The microbial strains used to evaluate antimicrobial activity included both gram-positive (S. aureus) and gram-negative (P. aeruginosa, S. typhi, and K. pneumoniae) types. These strains were purchased from the Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC) at the Institute of Microbial Technology in Chandigarh, India. The ultrapure water used in the experiments was produced using a Milli-Q ultrapure water system manufactured by Millipore in Burlington, MA, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from liquid whey
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CQDs were developed from liquid whey through a simple hydrothermal process. The LW-CQDs were synthesized by following the method outlined by Koutamehr et al. (2023). In brief, 100 ml of untreated liquid whey was stirred thoroughly at 400 rpm by using a magnetic stirrer (Sartorius India Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai India and M3D, Eltak DIGIMAG, India) for 20 min at room temperature (27 °C) to ensure complete dispersion. The resulting carbon solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave hydrothermal (Techinstro, Nagpur, India) of 100 ml quantity, tightly sealed, and heated at 200 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. After that, the obtained brownish-yellow solution was collected by a syringe of 10 ml and then subjected to centrifuge at 6000×g for 20 min in a normal centrifuge (Model R8C from REMI Laboratory Instruments, Mumbai, India) and filtered through a 3 KDa dialysis membrane to eliminate impurities or larger substances. The clear brownish-yellow solution (LW-CQDs) with fluorescent properties was collected using a glass syringe in the quartz vials to prevent any further reaction and stored in a refrigerator at 4–7 °C temperature until needed. For further characterization and application, the LW-CQDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter during sample preparation.
The LW-CQDs obtained from liquid whey underwent characterization through a variety of spectroscopic techniques.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum was employed to validate the synthesis of liquid whey-derived carbon quantum dots as described by the method of Boruah et al. (2020). The UV-vis spectrophotometer of Shimadzu UV-2700i, Kyoto, Japan, was utilized for analysis. The prepared sample of LW-CQDs (500 μl) was diluted with Milli-Q water (2.5 ml) for complete dispersion. The quartz cuvettes having a volume of 4 ml were filled with dispersed samples and one with Milli-Q water as a reference. The measurement was carried out at room temperature over a wavelength range from 800 nm to 200 nm. Subsequently, the spectrum was observed in a computer at different wavelengths by software (UV-probe).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential of LW-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Germany) was used for the analysis of average particle size and total surface charge distribution of LW-CQDs as mentioned by Hoan et al. (2019). The sample was prepared by homogenizing 500 μl of LW-CQDs with Milli-Q water using probe ultrasonication (Labman Scientific Instruments Ltd., Chennai, India) for 15 min before analysis. The sample was subjected to a particle size analyzer in a quartz cuvette of 4 ml volume. The results were analyzed using inbuilt software (Zetasizer version 7.02) in triplicate values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of LW-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional group analysis in LW-CQDs was carried out using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, following the procedure outlined by Kechagias et al. (2023). FT-IR analyses were conducted at room temperature using a Shimadzu IRTracer model spectrometer in Kyoto, Japan, operating within the mid-infrared spectrum from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The instrument consists diamond crystal cell ATR that was operated with a resolution of 0.5 cm−1 with the Spectrum 10 software for the measurement process. Briefly, 20 μl of LW-CQDs sample was mixed with dried 200 mg of KBr to loaded onto a sheet and further that sheet was subjected to a sample chamber for scanning. The sample of LW-CQDs was recorded in terms of wavenumber and transmittance in the generated spectrum. Moreover, the solvent isopropyl alcohol was used to clean the impurities from the ATR crystal for the maintenance of the instrument and future use.

The morphological image and elemental analysis of LW-CQDs were assessed as the method described by Mohammad et al. (2019). The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) utilized is of Thermo scientific Talos™ F200X G2 TEM, Waltham, Massachusetts, U.S. with applied accelerating voltage of 20 mV coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of Thermo Scientific™ at 10 mV was utilized for analysis. The copper grid has 10 μl of the liquid sample that was subjected to a sputtering process for 2 min at 30 mA voltage to make the sample conductive. The results in the form of images were finally obtained at different magnifications by using Thermo Scientific™ Velox software.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was utilized to investigate the phase-change behaviors and thermal characteristics of LW-CQDs, following the methodology proposed by Murru et al. (2022), by utilizing a TA Instruments Q20 differential scanning calorimeter. These measurements were performed under a nitrogen gas atmosphere from 25 to 350 °C with a scanning rate of 10 °C/min to prevent the sample from oxidation. The scanning started at a temperature of 20 °C, which was close to room temperature, helping in establishing a stable baseline. This was important for accurately measuring the heat flow associated with the material's transitions. The parameters, including melting peak temperature, melting enthalpy, onset temperature, and endset temperature were determined through DSC thermal analysis.

Fluorescence spectroscopic analysis was carried out following the procedure described by Alamdari et al. (2023). The sample was excited at 260 nm with a scanning rate of 10 nm min⁻1, covering the wavelength range of 800−350 nm with a power voltage of 120 kW. The instrument F-2500 spectrophotometer, specifically the PerkinElmer Spectrum FL version 1.0.0 from Germany, equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette was used. The slit widths for emission and excitation were configured at 5 and 2 nm, respectively. To record the photoluminescent spectra, the prepared solution of CQDs was placed into a cuvette with a 3.5 ml volume and a 3.5 cm path length. Subsequently, the peak was observed within the generated graph. The spectrum was observed in a computer at different wavelengths by software (Spectrum FLR version 1.1).

The antimicrobial properties of the synthesized fluorescence LW-CQDs were evaluated against S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, S. typhi, and K. pneumoniae using a modified well diffusion method in triplicates by following the CLSI (Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute) 2023 guidelines as outlined by Korah et al. (2023). To maintain a sterile environment, both the Petri dish and the sample were sterilized at 120 °C for 30 min in an autoclave before conducting the antimicrobial testing. A freshly prepared bacterial inoculum was evenly distributed across the surface of the nutrient agar medium using a sterilized cotton swab to ensure uniform bacteria distribution. The testing solution was prepared by mixing the LW-CQDs stock solution with sterile Milli-Q water. Subsequently, 0.01 μg/ml of LW-CQDs was introduced into wells created in the agar medium. Along with LW-CQDs, the chloramphenicol antibiotic and DMSO were used as positive and negative control, respectively, and then poured into the wells. The loaded plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After the incubation period, the inhibition zone (mm) formed in the Petri dish was observed. In addition, the broth microdilution assay was used to determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) against bacterial strains. A serial dilution concentration of LW-CQDs was prepared in nutrient agar and poured into microtiter plates. Furthermore, plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the results of MIC and MBC were observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time kill kinetic study of LW-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the antimicrobial efficacy of Liquid whey-derived carbon quantum dots (LW-CQDs) against pathogenic bacteria, we conducted the time-kill kinetic study as the method described by Divsalar et al. (2023). The Müller-Hinton broth was employed as the growth medium, as it is a widely recognized standard for antimicrobial susceptibility testing. In this study, varying concentrations of the LW-CQDs were introduced to bacterial cultures, with predetermined time intervals. The bacterial load was then quantified through viable cell count assessments. The assessment of bacterial growth involved the analysis of the logarithm of colony-forming units per milliliter (log CFU/mL) at each designated time point of 0, 18, 24, and 48 h. The results obtained from this time-kill kinetic study contribute valuable data to the understanding of the antimicrobial properties of LW-CQDs, thereby advancing the field of nanomaterial-based therapeutic interventions against pathogenic bacteria.

The photocatalytic dye reduction potential of LW-CQDs catalyst was evaluated with brilliant red dye using the procedure suggested by Alshammari et al. (2023) with some modifications. The degradation efficacy of dye was investigated under exposure to visible light (LED lamp with 200 W power) in the presence of a catalyst. Before light exposure, the suspension, which contained the dye and catalyst, underwent magnetic stirring at 250 rpm for 30 min under dark conditions to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Briefly, 100 ml of 10 ppm diluted dye solution and 15 ml of catalyst were added. After that suspension was irradiated with visible light under continuous stirring for 20–25 min. The control run of diluted dye solution without the presence of LW-CQDs was conducted simultaneously under visible light. Throughout the experiment, 2 ml of dye samples were taken at specific time intervals (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min) and subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. After centrifugation, the dye concentration was analyzed by spectrophotometer to monitor the absorption spectra in the range of 800-400 nm.

The following equation calculates the percentage degradation of brilliant red dye:
(1)
D y e
d e g r a d a t i o n
( % )
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
× 100
where C₀ denotes the initial concentration of the dye and Cₜ represents the remaining dye concentration at a specific time interval.

All the experiments were conducted in three replicates and the outcomes are presented as the average value ± the standard deviation (SD). Statistical evaluations were carried out through ANOVA, utilizing Microsoft® Excel 2016 (Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, WA) for data analysis, with a significance threshold set at p < 0.05. Graphical representations of the data were created using OriginPro version 8.5 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA).

As previously mentioned, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be derived from a diverse range of organic and inorganic sources. This study focused on the synthesis and characterization of LW-CQDs, where these carbon quantum dots were produced from readily available, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly dairy waste, specifically liquid whey.

In this study, the synthesis of CQDs employed liquid whey as the carbon source, chosen for its carboxyl groups that play a role in forming carbon bonds on the surfaces of CQDs. The synthesis of LW-CQDs involved hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 12 h. In this process, deionized water serves as the reaction medium for the synthesis of LW-CQDs. Carbon precursors undergo a reaction in a hydrothermal reactor at high temperatures, facilitating an oxidation process that enhances the fluorescence properties. This procedure involves heating carbon precursors under high pressure, promoting interactions among the precursors and water molecules. Under controlled high-pressure conditions (up to 3 MPa) using a hydrothermal, the reaction mixture experiences an increase in pressure as heat is supplied. This rise in pressure leads to the hydrolysis of water molecules. The reaction generates hydrogen ions (H+) and hydroxyl ions (OH−), causing repulsions between carbon layers and the formation of hydrogen bonds due to the interaction between water and carbon materials. As the heating process increases, the color of the LW-CQD solution transitioned to light brown, indicative of a gradual modification in the surface morphology, resulting in the formation of LW-CQDs. The development of hydrophilic groups in the structure of LW-CQDs allowed for their effective dispersion in aqueous solutions, making them well-suited for applications in wastewater treatment. The self-assembly process of LW-CQDs is influenced by hydrogen bonding interactions, particularly due to the electronegativity of hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O) atoms. As the concentration of LW-CQDs increases, the distance of hydroxyl (OH–) groups are reduced, facilitating the formation of (O-HO-) bonds. The H atoms on OH– groups, having a single electron available for covalent bonding to an O atom, contribute to the ease of electron attraction, given the higher electronegativity of oxygen. This leads to the formation of hydrogen bonds, bringing positive and negative charges together and promoting the self-assembly of LW-CQDs during hydrothermal treatment, resulting in the formation of nanoparticle-sized LW-CQDs and potentially porous spheres. This study is well supported by the outcomes of Koutamehr et al. (2023) with slight differences in application and characterization techniques including utilization of different precursors i.e. sour whey with a particle size of 7.8 nm by using TEM and showed antimicrobial activity against only two microorganisms. In comparison with that, we observed the particle size distribution of 9.04 nm by DLS (dynamic light spectrum) which is more sensitive than TEM. In addition, the LW-CQDs showed more effective results of antimicrobial analysis against four microorganisms with the application of photocatalytic dye reduction of brilliant red dye.

Results of optical properties are represented in Fig. 1 A, wherein, the LW-CQDs revealed a spectrum within the range of 800–200 nm exhibiting two distinct absorption peaks, located at approximately 249 and 293 nm. The 249 nm peak is associated with the π-π* transition of the C = C bond. The absorption peak at 293 nm is due to the n-π* transition related to the C = O bond, accompanied by electron transitions within the carbon dots containing oxygen. When the sample was exposed to UV light with a longer wavelength of 375 nm, the supernatant solution quickly emitted a blue color, as shown in the respective figure. These findings align with the results reported by Elizabeth et al. (2023), wherein the UV-Vis spectrum of Crescentia cujete fruit waste derived CQDs displayed a broad absorption peak around 220 and 280 nm, possibly arising from the π–π* electronic transition of C-O groups in the CQDs, with a smaller peak at 330 nm due to n-π* excitation in the carboxyl functional group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size, PDI (poly dispersity index), and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Results illustrated in Fig. 1B and C, the particle size distribution of LW-CQDs was characterized by conducting and it was observed that LW-CQDs showed an average particle size of 9.04 ± 0.76 nm and a zeta potential of −27 mV, respectively. This observation indicates that there is a substantial electrostatic repulsion between similarly charged LW-CQDs, which is significantly stronger than the attractive forces. This electrostatic repulsion ensures the stability of the dispersed system, preventing aggregation. The negative charge on the particles induces this electrostatic repulsion, which prevents clumping and maintains dispersion stability. To further assess the stability and uniformity of the LW-CQDs, the Polydispersity Index (PDI) was determined. The measured PDI value of 0.309 indicates that the LW-CQDs exhibited a generally uniform distribution in the suspension. A higher PDI value indicates a broader range of particle sizes, while a lower PDI suggests a more uniform size distribution. According to reported literature, PDI values closer to 0 indicate monodisperse particles, while values closer to 1 suggest polydisperse particles. This finding is consistent with the findings of Anthony et al. (2020), who observed a similar trend in PDI of CQDs derived from ice biryani using the hydrothermal method.

Results of FTIR are represented in Fig. 1D, wherein the functional group and surface structure analysis of the synthesized LW-CQDs were conducted. The results obtained indicated prominent absorption bands at 2146.74 cm⁻1 and 1637.64 cm⁻1, which could be attributed to the C-C stretching vibration of methyl or methylene groups within the CQDs. They can contribute to the stability of the LW-CQDs by preventing aggregation through steric hindrance. Furthermore, a weaker absorption band was observed at 3306.15 cm⁻1, suggesting the presence of hydroxyl groups. These groups increased the hydrophilicity of LW-CQDs, thereby improving their solubility in water and other polar solvents. Another peak was obtained at 1074.00 cm⁻1, which was attributed to the carbonyl group (C-O) and highlights the chemical diversity of the LW-CQD surface. These identified functional groups are indicative of the distinctive characteristics of the LW-CQDs, specifically incorporating CH3 and OH groups. Such functionalization reveals that these LW-CQDs are suitable for sensor applications by participating in specific interactions with target analytes. This finding aligns with previous research by Chaudhary et al. (2022), who similarly reported the presence of hydroxy, alkoxy, oxygen, and other functionalities in CQDs derived from bike pollutant soot.

The HR-TEM micrograph of LW-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1E. The high-resolution TEM image demonstrates that the LW-CQDs are characterized by their small size, uniform distribution, and spherical shape. The average size of the LW-CQDs was measured to be 0.58 ± 0.07 nm. In Fig. 1F, the EDS spectrum of the LW-CQDs reveals the elemental composition of the sample, with 21.14 wt% carbon and 78.86 wt% oxygen. The HR-TEM analysis indicates that these CQDs exhibit a structure resembling graphite. Notably, this method represents the first reported approach for synthesizing CQDs from liquid whey, yielding a high purity of carbon of 21.14 wt%. These findings align with the research of Zhu et al. (2019), who reported a size distribution of 7–9 nm through TEM analysis and analyzed elements such as C, O, N, etc., in fluorescent tea and peanut shell-derived carbon quantum dots using EDS.

The results of the thermal stability of LW-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1G. Herein, only one exothermic peak was observed during analysis. The sample was scanned to analyze the phase transition of LW-CQDs which was observed at 102.63 °C. The obtained peak might be due to evaporation occurring in water molecules present in the material. There was no presence of a peak in the thermograph under 100 °C which shows the thermal stability of LW-CQDs. It can be seen in the DSC curve that pure LW-CQDs exhibit onset and endset temperatures at 98.29 and 120.94 °C. Our results are similar to the results of Lauria and Lizundia (2020) showing the degradation of PLA (Polylactide) pellets followed by an endothermic peak at 180.9 °C with a heat enthalpy change at 72.2 °C because of the presence of shorter chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy and stability of LW-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As represented in Fig. 1H, the emission and excitation spectra of LW-CQDs were analyzed. These LW-CQDs appear yellowish in an aqueous solution under natural light and emit strong blue fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light. When excited at 240 nm, the fluorescence spectra indicate a peak emission at 418 nm. Fig. 1I demonstrates the stability of LW-CQDs when exposed to UV light. The stability of LW-CQDs was demonstrated by the consistent intensity of the emission peak over 120 min. This photoluminescent property makes LW-CQDs promising fluorescent probes for sensing applications, with potential analytical uses. Our observation is in agreement with the results reported by Ren et al. (2023), who also found stable emission characteristics in fluorescent CQDs derived from waste peach leaves. These dots emitted light at a wavelength (λem) of 440 nm when excited at a wavelength (λex) of 360 nm, as determined through fluorescence spectroscopy.

The overview of the antibacterial characteristics of LW-CQDs showcases their effectiveness against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria as shown in Fig. 2 A. Positive control (chloramphenicol) revealed a significantly higher zone of inhibition (27.04 ± 0.06 mm), while, notably, the LW-CQDs showed a significantly (p < 0.05) higher zone of inhibition against gram positive S. aureus (25.98 ± 0.17 mm) than that of gram-negative bacteria counterparts. The zone of inhibition for K. pneumoniae, S. typhi, and P. aeruginosa was found to be 24.30 ± 0.13, 24.18 ± 0.52, and 24.54 ± 0.12 mm, respectively. In addition, Fig. 2B illustrates the data of MIC and MBC of LW-CQDs against each bacterial strain. The MIC values for K. pneumoniae, S. typhi, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus were 6.69 ± 0.43, 7.79 ± 0.16, 7.03 ± 0.23 and 4.47 ± 0.01 μl/ml, respectively. In contrast, the observed MBC values were 3.91 ± 0.09, 4.46 ± 0.28, 4.61 ± 0.13 and 2.78 ± 0.09 μl/ml, respectively. The observed ability of LW-CQDs to inhibit microbial growth is attributed to the presence of a variety of functional groups within these quantum dots. These functional groups may interfere with cellular enzyme functions and hinder the process of cellular proliferation. Furthermore, the structure of LW-CQDs, characterized by an extensive π-conjugated readily interfaces with bacterial cell walls through electron transfer. The antimicrobial mechanism of LW-CQDs is widely speculated in the literature, involving electrostatic interactions, reactive oxygen species (ROS), or exposure to light. ROS generation, particularly hydroxyl radicals and carboxyl groups identified through FTIR and EDS studies, plays a crucial role in antimicrobial activity. The presence of positively charged hydrogen elements on LW-CQDs enables them to adhere to negatively charged microbes. When LW-CQDs penetrate the cell membrane, they ultimately result in the death of these microorganisms. This study highlights the inhibition of LW-CQDs against K. pneumoniae, S. typhi, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus suggest their potential application in the food industry. Numerous studies have documented the antimicrobial effectiveness of hydrogen and carbon-containing CQDs against various microorganisms. For instance, Kaur et al. (2023) reported on CQDs/silver nanocomposites that efficiently generate superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, exhibiting interactions with Escherichia coli bacteria (Gram-negative bacteria).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time kill kinetic study of LW-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: This research study investigates the antimicrobial efficacy of LW-CQDs against selected pathogenic microorganisms, by employing time-kill kinetic analysis outlined in Table 1 . Herein LW-CQDs showed significantly higher killing effects against S. aureus as compared to other microbial counterparts. The observed CFU/mL values for K. pneumoniae (7.66 ± 0.46 log CFU/mL), S. typhi (7.74 ± 0.19 log CFU/mL), P. aeruginosa (7.78 ± 0.35 log CFU/mL), and S. aureus (7.97 ± 0.43 log CFU/mL) highlight the potent bactericidal capabilities of LW-CQDs. Herein, the CQDs may interacted with microbial cell membranes, disrupting their structural integrity. This interaction can lead to increased permeability, release of intracellular contents, and eventual cell death. Additionally, the CQDs may generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) within the microbial cells (Zulkarnain et al., 2020). These ROS induce oxidative stress, causing damage to cellular components such as proteins, DNA, and lipids. As the exposure time increases, the cumulative effect of membrane disruption and oxidative stress intensify, resulting in a higher antimicrobial potency. Time-kill kinetic studies provide valuable insights into the dynamic nature of the antimicrobial activity of food-derived CQDs, helping to optimize their application in various fields, including food preservation (Nie et al., 2020). Overall, based on the findings, synthesized LW-CQDs could be widely used to develop various antimicrobial components including packaging film, cosmetic and pharmaceutic products.

Herein, Fig. 3 A illustrates the UV-vis absorption spectra of the brilliant red solution while exposed to LW-CQDs, with varying durations of light illumination. The analysis of the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized sample involved the use of brilliant red as a model organic pollutant dye under visible light. The results indicate a significant photocatalytic effect, with a calculated degradation efficiency of 92.95 % for brilliant red dye after 30 min of visible light exposure. Initially, there was no observable degradation. However, upon initiating the visible light exposure in the photocatalytic reaction, the degradation rate of LW-CQDs reached 50% within the first 15 min. Subsequently, the degradation rate increased to 92% after 30 min of visible light irradiation. The degradation efficiency for brilliant red dye by LW-CQDs under visible light is shown in Fig. 3B. Our results were aligned with the study performed by Montazer et al. (2023), which demonstrated a 99.7% reduction in malachite green within 12 min when exposed to visible light in the presence of a nanocomposite consisting of cuttlebone, carbon quantum dots, and nickel oxide.

An environmentally friendly and efficient approach for the fabrication of fluorescent CQDs using a hydrothermal method, with liquid whey serving as the precursor material, has been developed. The resulting LW-CQDs exhibit strong fluorescence and excellent stability. These CQDs exhibit a broad UV–visible absorption spectrum and display excitation wavelength-independent fluorescent properties. An analysis using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) has shown that the synthesized LW-CQDs have a high level of purity, measured at 21%. Additionally, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis has confirmed the crystalline nature of these LW-CQDs, revealing an average particle size of 0.58 ± 0.07 nm. Remarkably, LW-CQDs demonstrate significant antimicrobial activity against various bacterial strains. In particular, they exhibit promising antimicrobial potential against K. pneumoniae, S. typhi, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus with a substantial inhibition zone and time-kill kinetic study. Furthermore, LW-CQDs exhibit remarkable photocatalytic properties, effectively removing 92.95 % of brilliant red dye under visible light within just 30 min. These highly crystalline and fluorescent LW-CQDs hold significant potential for applications in biosensors, biomedical imaging, drug delivery, and solar cells. The LW-CQDs, while demonstrating promising antimicrobial and photocatalytic properties, may face limitations in the food sector due to potential concerns about their long-term biocompatibility and toxicity, which require thorough investigation. Additionally, the scalability of their production using liquid whey and their integration into food packaging or preservation systems pose challenges that need to be addressed. Future research should focus on assessing the safety of LW-CQDs in food-related applications, optimizing their synthesis for large-scale production, and exploring innovative ways to incorporate them into food systems for enhanced safety and quality.
The research was funded through the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R379), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.

Sweezee Thakur: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Aarti Bains: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Anil Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Gulden Goksen: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Mudasir Yaqoob: Methodology, Formal analysis. Mohammad Khalid Parvez: Resources, Funding acquisition. Mohammed S. Al-Dosari: Resources, Funding acquisition. Prince Chawla: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R379), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for supporting this study.
